description: Someone is 'pokera' when they can perform a stated activity to its criterion under present conditions. A joiner may be competent with a familiar hand plane and not yet competent with a borrowed lathe. 'thesani' (skill) is learned capacity held between performances, while 'kiranu' (qualified) reports prerequisites met under a source. 'nukesa' (expert) reaches beyond one criterion into sustained domain practice.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release them into true-mid rounded back 'o'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release it into stressed true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip for 'r' and finish with open central 'a'.
usage_notes: A nearby clause can give the activity and criterion; evidence supports the judgement. Competence is the noun reading. Permission to act comes from 'kanuwa' (authorize), not from demonstrated ability.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A result on one task stays a result on one task; it does not become a verdict on the person's worth or authority.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A craftsperson can demonstrate competence with the actual tool and material even when no institution has issued a credential.

semantic_domains.activity: present ability to perform a stated activity to its criterion
semantic_domains.cognition: a judgement of practical capacity grounded in evidence
